cataloging_subject.label: The Canterbury Tales (Harley MS 7334/Furnivall representation)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's unfinished Middle English pilgrimage frame and conventional twenty-four-tale collection in the order and readings of British Library Harley MS 7334, as edited by Frederick James Furnivall in 1885 and represented by U-M/CME item AGZ8246.0001.001. The boundary also exposes, but does not count as additional Canterbury tales, the anonymous non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn transmitted after the Cook's Tale; prologues, links, and the Retraction; a separately sourced Royal MS 17 D XV Nun's Priest end-link; the bilingual Angelus ad Virginem from Arundel MS 248; and two W. H. Hooper woodcut suites copied from Ellesmere MS EL 26 C 9 and Cambridge MS Gg.4.27. The abstract work, manuscript-specific readings, printed edition, page-image carriers, digital encodings, repository XML, and appendix sources remain distinct lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Canterbury Tales
catalog_summary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer; anonymous responsibility for the non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn and Angelus ad Virginem appendix
catalog_summary.date: Canterbury Tales composed chiefly in the late 1380s and 1390s; Harley MS 7334 c. 1405-1415; source edition 1885
catalog_summary.region: England; Harley witness probably London or East Anglia

notes: The Harley MS 7334/Furnivall qualifier defines this cataloging representation; it is not part of the conventional work title.

titles.value: The Harleian MS. 7334 of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
notes: Capitalization is normalized for display from the edition and U-M bibliographic title.

agents.name: Anonymous author of Gamelyn
notes: Local responsibility placeholder for an unidentified medieval author; it does not imply collective composition.

agents.name: Anonymous Latin author of Angelus ad Virginem
notes: Local responsibility placeholder for the unidentified author of the Latin hymn printed as the original; it does not imply collective composition.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English translator of Angelus ad Virginem
notes: Local responsibility placeholder for the unidentified maker of the Middle English rendering; it does not imply collective translation.

agents.name: William Harcourt Hooper
notes: British wood-engraver, 1834-1912; the 1885 carrier credits him as the copier and cutter of both appendix suites.

scope.description: The ten sequence containers representing the conventional Canterbury Tales fragments, including their prologues, links, and Retraction; explicitly excludes the nested non-Chaucerian Gamelyn appendix and all edition appendices

assertion.notes: Furnivall explicitly calls the tale spurious in relation to Chaucer; no named author is assigned.

The Latin original printed in the right-hand column
The Middle English rendering printed in the left-hand column
Visual copying and wood engraving of the thirty-two design-bearing plate surfaces

notes: The schema has no engraver role. Adapter is used only for Hooper's explicitly credited visual copying and block cutting, not for textual authorship or for creation of the medieval exemplar designs.

date_statements.display: Begun probably in the late 1380s and worked on through the 1390s; unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400

scope.description: The Chaucerian core represented by the ten fragment containers; explicitly excludes the nested Gamelyn appendix and all edition appendices

notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame; this range is not assigned to Gamelyn.

Furnivall's headings, notes, foreword, and source apparatus

form_statements.description: Predominantly verse, with the prose Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale; Gamelyn is verse and does not change the mixed whole-work classification.

scope.description: The tale-telling journey and links across the Chaucerian fragments; excludes Gamelyn and edition appendices
Social and occupational portraiture and contest in the frame, pilgrim prologues, and links

subject_statements.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: Pilgrims traveling toward Canterbury tell stories in many forms and social voices. This record preserves the particular sequence and textual state of Harley MS 7334, including the non-Chaucerian Gamelyn after the incomplete Cook's Tale and an eight-leaf loss that removes the Squire-Franklin transition and the opening 487 Franklin lines. Its edition-defined whole also includes a Royal MS 17 D XV end-link, the bilingual Arundel MS 248 Angelus ad Virginem, and issued Ellesmere and Gg.4.27 woodcut suites, each bound to its separate source rather than attributed to Harley.

Harley order: A/I, B-alpha/II, D/V, E/VI, F/VII, G/VIII, C/IV, B-beta/III, H/IX, I/X. The U-M XML transcribes twenty-four Chaucerian tales plus Gamelyn, omits broad front and back matter, represents the Parson analysis as an empty omitted division, and retains complete page, folio, and Six-Text milestone systems with documented source anomalies.

extent.description: Twenty-four conventional Chaucerian tales are counted: twenty-two verse and two prose. The 706 LG and 16,772 L counts are semantic XML element counts after excluding Gamelyn's eight LG and 902 L elements; they still include verse prologues and links and are not a critical lineation. The 64-part human-readable inventory preserves all 49 main-text DIV2 boundaries, ten Harley-order group containers, a separately exposed Retraction within DIV2 12.3, and four edition appendices. Raw XML metrics are 13 DIV1, 49 DIV2, 41 DIV3, 714 LG, 17,674 L, 217 P, 1,103 MILESTONE, and 778 PB elements. Prologues, links, Retraction, edition appendices, and the non-Chaucerian Gamelyn do not increment the tale count.

content_parts.label: Group A, Fragment I
content_parts.summary: General Prologue; Knight, Miller, Reeve, incomplete Cook, then the separately anonymous Gamelyn in Harley order.

content_parts.label: General Prologue
content_parts.summary: Pilgrimage frame, pilgrim portraits, and tale-telling agreement.

content_parts.label: The Knight's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse romance of Palamon, Arcite, and Emelye.

content_parts.label: Miller's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Miller interrupts the ordered tale sequence and introduces his fabliau.

content_parts.label: The Miller's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of Nicholas, Alison, John, and Absolon.

content_parts.label: Reeve's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Reeve answers the Miller and introduces his retaliatory tale.

content_parts.label: The Reeve's Tale
content_parts.summary: Retaliatory fabliau of two clerks and a miller.

content_parts.label: Cook's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Cook reacts to the Reeve's tale and introduces his own.

content_parts.label: The Cook's Tale
content_parts.summary: Incomplete fabliau of Perkyn Revel.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Gamelyn
content_parts.summary: Anonymous 902-line Middle English outlaw romance transmitted after the incomplete Cook's Tale and explicitly labeled a spurious appendix by Furnivall. It is not Chaucerian and does not increment the conventional twenty-four-tale Canterbury Tales count.

content_parts.label: Group B-alpha, Fragment II
content_parts.summary: Man of Law sequence.

content_parts.label: Man of Law's Head-link and Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Host calls on the Man of Law, whose prologue precedes the Constance narrative.

content_parts.label: The Man of Law's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale of Constance.

content_parts.label: Group D, Fragment V
content_parts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence.

content_parts.label: Wife of Bath's Preamble and Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Wife's autobiographical marriage argument, including the Friar-Summoner wrangle before her tale.

content_parts.label: The Wife of Bath's Tale
content_parts.summary: Arthurian romance of marriage and sovereignty.

content_parts.label: Friar's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Friar introduces his attack on summoners.

content_parts.label: The Friar's Tale
content_parts.summary: Clerical satire of a corrupt summoner and a devil.

content_parts.label: Summoner's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Summoner answers the Friar and introduces his anti-fraternal tale.

content_parts.label: The Summoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Anti-fraternal fabliau.

content_parts.label: Group E, Fragment VI
content_parts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence.

content_parts.label: Clerk's Head-link and Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Host calls on the Clerk, whose prologue introduces Griselda.

content_parts.label: The Clerk's Tale
content_parts.summary: Six-part verse tale of Griselda with Chaucer's envoy.

content_parts.label: Merchant's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Merchant responds to the Clerk's marriage tale.

content_parts.label: The Merchant's Tale
content_parts.summary: Marriage tale with fabliau elements.

content_parts.label: Merchant-Squire Link
content_parts.summary: The Host turns from the Merchant to the Squire.

content_parts.label: Group F, Fragment VII
content_parts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence interrupted by an eight-leaf manuscript loss.

content_parts.label: Squire's Head-link
content_parts.summary: The Squire begins his turn after the preceding Merchant-Squire link.

content_parts.label: The Squire's Tale
content_parts.summary: Incomplete romance; the Harley text stops before an eight-leaf manuscript loss.

content_parts.label: The Franklin's Tale
content_parts.summary: Only the surviving tail is present; the Squire-Franklin link and Franklin lines 1-487 are absent and not supplied.

locators.value: resumes at f. 157v; f. 157r and seven preceding leaves lost

content_parts.label: Group G, Fragment VIII
content_parts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence.

content_parts.label: The Second Nun's Tale
content_parts.summary: Saint's life of Cecilia with prologue.

content_parts.label: Canon's Yeoman Prologue, Opening
content_parts.summary: Opening portion of the Canon's Yeoman's arrival and introduction.

content_parts.label: Canon's Yeoman Preamble, Continuation
content_parts.summary: Continuation of the Canon's Yeoman's preamble before the alchemical tale.

content_parts.label: The Canon's Yeoman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Alchemy and fraud narrative.

content_parts.label: Group C, Fragment IV
content_parts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence.

content_parts.label: The Physician's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale of Virginia.

content_parts.label: Physician-Pardoner Introduction
content_parts.summary: The Host responds to the Physician and calls on the Pardoner.

content_parts.label: Pardoner's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Pardoner exposes his preaching methods and avarice.

content_parts.label: The Pardoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Exemplum of three rioters seeking Death.

content_parts.label: Group B-beta, Fragment III
content_parts.summary: Shipman through Nun's Priest sequence.

content_parts.label: The Shipman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of a merchant, wife, and monk.

content_parts.label: Host-Prioress Link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls on the Prioress after the Shipman's tale.

content_parts.label: The Prioress's Tale
content_parts.summary: Miracle-of-the-Virgin narrative.

content_parts.label: Prioress-Thopas Link
content_parts.summary: The Host turns from the Prioress to Chaucer the pilgrim.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Sir Thopas
content_parts.summary: Parodic romance interrupted by the Host.

content_parts.label: Thopas-Melibee Interruption and Introduction
content_parts.summary: The Host stops Sir Thopas and Chaucer introduces Melibee.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Melibee
content_parts.summary: Prose moral tale; Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraph breaks because Harley supplies neither lineation nor paragraphing.

content_parts.label: Monk's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Host's response to Melibee and the Monk's introduction.

content_parts.label: The Monk's Tale
content_parts.summary: Series of tragedies.

content_parts.label: Monk-Nun's Priest Link
content_parts.summary: The Monk is interrupted and the Nun's Priest is called to tell a merry tale.

content_parts.label: The Nun's Priest's Tale
content_parts.summary: Beast fable of Chauntecleer and Pertelote; Harley lacks the separately appended end-link.

content_parts.label: Group H, Fragment IX
content_parts.summary: Manciple sequence.

content_parts.label: Manciple's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Manciple mocks the Cook and introduces his tale.

content_parts.label: The Manciple's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale of Apollo and the crow.

content_parts.label: Group I, Fragment X
content_parts.summary: Parson sequence and Retraction; the printed analytical contents are represented only by an omitted XML division.

content_parts.label: Blank-Parson Link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls on the Parson for the final tale.

content_parts.label: Contents and Analysis of the Parson's Tale
content_parts.summary: Printed analytical contents on pp. 593-597, represented by an empty XML division explicitly marked omitted.

locators.value: AGZ8246.0001.001:12.2; PB 607-611; no encoded P, L, or MILESTONE content

content_parts.label: The Parson's Tale
content_parts.summary: Prose penitential treatise; Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraphing.

content_parts.label: Chaucer's Retraction
content_parts.summary: Closing retraction after the Parson's Tale.

content_parts.label: Nun's Priest's End-link appendix
content_parts.summary: Fifteen-line end-link printed from Royal MS 17 D XV, f. 284r, explicitly described as absent from Harley MS 7334.

content_parts.label: Angelus ad Virginem appendix
content_parts.summary: Facsimile and facing Middle English translation and Latin original of the Annunciation hymn mentioned at Miller A3216, copied from Arundel MS 248, f. 154r; both anonymous language components are distinct from the Harley text.

locators.value: unnumbered facsimile and pp. 695-696; Middle English left column, Latin original right column

content_parts.label: Twenty-three Ellesmere teller woodcuts
content_parts.summary: William Harcourt Hooper's 23 copied-and-cut teller portraits, in carrier order and captions: The Knight, The Miller, The Reeve, The Cook, The Man of Law, The Shipman, The Prioress, Chaucer, The Monk, The Nun's Priest, The Doctor, The Pardoner, The Wife of Bath, The Friar, The Summoner, The Clerk of Oxford, The Merchant, The Squire, The Franklin, The Second Nun, The Canon's Yeoman, The Manciple, and The Parson. They are visual apparatus copied from Ellesmere MS EL 26 C 9, not Harley textual evidence.

locators.value: suite title PDF 719; 23 design-bearing surfaces on odd PDF 721-765
suite title PDF 721; 23 design-bearing surfaces on odd PDF 723-767

locators.scheme: IIIF canvas numbers in carrier order

content_parts.label: Gg.4.27 teller and allegorical woodcuts
content_parts.summary: William Harcourt Hooper's copies of 12 figures on nine design-bearing plate surfaces: six teller surfaces captioned The Reeve, The Cook of London, The Wife of Bath, The Pardoner, The Monk, and The Manciple, followed by three paired allegory surfaces captioned Envy and Charity, Gluttony and Abstinence, and Lechery and Chastity. These are visual apparatus from Cambridge MS Gg.4.27, not Harley textual evidence.

locators.value: suite title PDF 767; distinct surfaces PDF 769 Reeve, 771 Cook, 773 Wife, 775 Pardoner, 777 Monk, 787 Manciple, 789 Envy/Charity, 791 Gluttony/Abstinence, 793 Lechery/Chastity; PDF 779/781/783 repeat the Pardoner surface and PDF 785 repeats Monk

all nine distinct surfaces PDF 769, 771, 773, 775, 777, 779, 781, 783, 785 in the same design order; PDF 787 repeats the Ellesmere Canon's Yeoman surface; the separate Gg suite title leaf is not visible in this scan
Reeve f. 186r; Cook f. 192v; Wife f. 222r; Pardoner f. 306r; Monk f. 352r; Manciple f. 395r; Envy/Charity f. 416r; Gluttony/Abstinence f. 432r; Lechery/Chastity f. 433r

locators.scheme: IIIF canvas numbers by figure or pair

evidence.citation: Pinned CME source XML for agz8246 at submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Exact repository source for title, U-M and print imprints, OCR/TEI declaration, division hierarchy, text, page breaks, and milestone systems.

evidence.citation: Complete structural and milestone audit of the pinned agz8246 XML.
evidence.locator: all DIV1/DIV2/DIV3, PB, folio MILESTONE, Six-Text MILESTONE, LG, L, P, NOTE1, and TRAILER elements
evidence.summary: Confirms 13 DIV1, 49 DIV2, 41 DIV3, 714 LG, 17,674 L, 217 P, 1,103 MILESTONE, and 778 PB elements; maps all ten Harley-order groups and every tale; verifies PB SEQ 1-778 and TIFF refs 00000001-00000778 without gaps; audits 572 folio markers with duplicated 72a/72b, absent 157a and 286b, and 531 unique Six-Text page markers with the Group F 496-to-514 jump aligned to the eight-leaf loss.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, The Harleian ms. 7334 of Chaucer's Canterbury tales, AGZ8246.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Current item title, authorship, 1885 bibliographic description, group contents, public-domain/No Copyright-United States statement, and access to the searchable text and page-image links.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 7334.
evidence.summary: Authoritative current evidence for shelfmark, c. 1405-1415 date, probable London or East Anglian origin, parchment codex, unique tale order, Gamelyn ff. 59r-70v, Chaucer text resuming f. 71r, Retraction ending f. 286r, physical access, and currently unavailable linked images.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The Harleian MS. 7334 of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales (London: Chaucer Society, 1885), complete Harvard/Google scan.
evidence.locator: complete 799-page carrier; title matter, foreword, all text, pp. 693-696 appendix, and thirty-two plates
evidence.summary: Direct inspection establishes Furnivall's Harley-only transcription claim, the unfilled eight-leaf loss, Tyrwhitt prose paragraphing, Six-Text comparison milestones, the Royal 17 D XV end-link, Arundel 248 hymn, and Ellesmere/Gg visual sources. The cached PDF is 26,393,042 bytes; an independently scanned complete 800-page copy is also retained.

evidence.citation: Norman Blake editions of The Canterbury Tales Project, Ha4 multitext and complete downloadable dataset.
evidence.locator: 54 Ha4 transcription files within 748-file ZIP; TG-Ha4-Est-3.txt includes Gamelyn
evidence.summary: Independent modern diplomatic transcription and folio-level comparison source for Harley 7334, distributed by the University of Sheffield under CC BY-NC 4.0. The complete eight-manuscript dataset ZIP is 10,127,140 bytes and passes ZIP CRC validation.

evidence.citation: Helen Cooper, The Canterbury Tales, 3rd ed., Oxford Guides to Chaucer, chapter 1, The Canterbury Tales (Oxford University Press, 2023).
evidence.summary: Supports the cautious late-1380s-through-1400 composition range, unfinished status, possibility that individual tales predate the frame, and broad genre and subject classifications.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, Fragments or Groups of Tales.
evidence.summary: Explains the ten internally linked fragments, unfinished state, and absence of a securely authorial final order.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, London, British Library, MS Harley 7334.
evidence.summary: Scholarly manuscript record locating Gamelyn in Harley 7334 and supporting its treatment as a distinct Middle English romance rather than a twenty-fifth Chaucerian tale.

evidence.citation: Complete plate-by-plate visual audit and source crosswalk for both independently scanned Furnivall 1885 carriers and the complete Ellesmere and Gg.4.27 IIIF sequences.
evidence.locator: Harvard/Google PDF 719-793; independent Google copy PDF 721-787; all 23 Ellesmere portraits and all 12 Gg figures; Huntington canvases 26-418 and CUDL canvases 383-881 as individually listed in the content-part locators
evidence.summary: Confirms every design, caption order, carrier page, exemplar folio, and IIIF canvas. The Harvard scan preserves all 32 distinct design-bearing plate surfaces but repeats three scan states of the Pardoner surface and one of Monk; the second scan preserves the same 32 surfaces compactly and later repeats Canon's Yeoman. Repeated scans are digitization states, not additional woodcuts. The suites are proven in the 1885 Harley carrier only and cannot be projected backward into earlier Corpus or Lansdowne part carriers.

evidence.citation: British Museum Collections Online, William Harcourt Hooper (BIOG32010).
evidence.summary: Official museum authority record identifies William Harcourt Hooper (1834-1912) as a British wood-engraver; the Furnivall carrier itself supplies the work-specific copied-and-cut attribution.

evidence.citation: Deutsche Nationalbibliothek GND 117540579, Furnivall, Frederick James.
evidence.summary: Official authority evidence for the editor's identity, life dates 1825-1910, and linked identifiers; cached JSON-LD is 8,096 bytes and CC0-marked metadata.

open_questions.question: Was the bound edition first issued late in 1885, or only after Furnivall dated the foreword 8 January 1886?
next_steps: Check Chaucer Society annual reports, issue wrappers, and contemporary accession records.
Retain the title-page imprint and dated foreword as separate evidence until issuance is documented.

open_questions.question: What defensible composition range should be assigned specifically to the anonymous Tale of Gamelyn represented here?
next_steps: Consult a current critical edition and manuscript stemma focused on Gamelyn.
Do not inherit Chaucer's 1387-1400 composition range for this separately authored item.

notes: Harley order is recorded as witness evidence, not presented as Chaucer's final authorial order.
The two duplicated folio labels, omitted f. 157r marker, absent terminal 286b, and Six-Text milestone gaps are preserved as layer-specific source facts rather than silently normalized.
The XML's 778 page breaks inventory the historical scan carrier; omitted front/back divisions mean page coverage is broader than encoded textual content.
